title: Kafka POC 3 — Consumer Groups, Partitioning & Ordering
summary: This POC builds an order-processing pipeline on Apache Kafka (KRaft mode, no ZooKeeper) with a Java producer and consumer. Orders are keyed by customer_id so each customer's orders stay ordered within a single partition, while multiple consumers in one group process partitions in parallel. It demonstrates the key→partition→ordering guarantee, consumer-group rebalancing, partition-count as the cap on parallelism, and pluggable partition-assignment strategies.

questions.question: What guarantees that all orders for a single customer are processed in order?
alternatives: Orders are timestamped and the consumer sorts them before processing
Each order uses the customer id as the key, so the same key always maps to the same partition, and a partition is ordered
The producer sets acks=all which forces global ordering across the topic
Kafka guarantees total ordering across all partitions of a topic
questions.comment: Kafka only guarantees ordering within a partition, not across a topic. The producer keys each record with `customer-N`; the default partitioner hashes the key so the same customer always lands on the same partition, preserving that customer's order. acks=all is about durability, not ordering.

questions.question: The topic `orders` has 6 partitions. What happens when a 7th consumer joins the group `order-processors`?
alternatives: Kafka automatically adds a 7th partition to balance the load
All 7 consumers share the 6 partitions cooperatively, each getting a fraction
The 7th consumer sits idle with no partitions assigned until one frees up
The group throws an error and refuses the new consumer
questions.comment: A partition is assigned to at most one consumer per group, so partition count caps active consumers. With 6 partitions, a 7th (or 9th) consumer gets nothing assigned and stays idle — exactly the README's 'why did adding a 9th consumer do nothing' question.

questions.question: Which Kafka mode does the docker-compose broker run in?
alternatives: KRaft mode — broker and controller roles, no ZooKeeper
Classic mode with a separate ZooKeeper container
Cluster mode with 3 broker replicas
Embedded mode inside the kafka-ui container
questions.comment: The compose sets KAFKA_PROCESS_ROLES=broker,controller and KAFKA_CONTROLLER_QUORUM_VOTERS, with no ZooKeeper service — that's KRaft mode, where Kafka manages its own metadata quorum.

questions.question: How does the producer decide which customer each order belongs to?
alternatives: It cycles sequentially customer-0 through customer-9
It reads the customer id from a command-line argument
It picks a random int 0..9 via ThreadLocalRandom and builds key `customer-<n>`
It uses the orderId modulo the partition count
questions.comment: ProducerApp calls `ThreadLocalRandom.current().nextInt(NUM_CUSTOMERS)` with NUM_CUSTOMERS=10, then forms the key `customer-` + n. Random keys still hash deterministically, so a given customer-N consistently maps to one partition.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the ConsumerRebalanceListener in ConsumerApp?
alternatives: It commits offsets to the database before shutdown
It logs which partitions are REVOKED and ASSIGNED when the group rebalances
It manually assigns partitions to each consumer by name
It throttles polling so consumers don't overload the broker
questions.comment: The listener overrides onPartitionsRevoked / onPartitionsAssigned to print `REVOKED`/`ASSIGNED` lines. This makes rebalances visible — when a consumer joins or dies, you see partitions move between consumers.

questions.question: A consumer with no assigned partitions prints `[ none - IDLE ]`. Where does that string come from?
alternatives: Kafka emits it automatically when a consumer is starved
The `partitionsOf` helper returns it when the partition collection is empty
It is the broker's response to a failed subscription
kafka-ui injects it into the consumer's log stream
questions.comment: `partitionsOf` checks `parts.isEmpty()` and returns the literal `[ none - IDLE ]`. It's a code-level signal that this consumer joined the group but got zero partitions (e.g., when consumers outnumber partitions).

questions.question: The consumer sets AUTO_OFFSET_RESET_CONFIG to "earliest". What does that control?
alternatives: How long the consumer waits before timing out a poll
Where a consumer starts reading when it has no committed offset for a partition
The maximum number of records returned per poll
Whether offsets are committed automatically or manually
questions.comment: auto.offset.reset only applies when there's no valid committed offset (new group or expired offset). "earliest" replays from the start of the partition; "latest" would skip to new messages. Auto-commit behavior is a separate setting (ENABLE_AUTO_COMMIT_CONFIG=true).

questions.question: The consumer reads ASSIGNMENT=range by default. What does the `assignor` method do with values like "sticky" or "cooperative"?
alternatives: Maps the short name to the fully-qualified assignor class (e.g. CooperativeStickyAssignor) for PARTITION_ASSIGNMENT_STRATEGY_CONFIG
Sets the number of partitions to create on the topic
Chooses between earliest and latest offset reset
Selects which consumer group the instance joins
questions.comment: The `assignor` switch maps short names to FQ class names — RangeAssignor, RoundRobinAssignor, StickyAssignor, CooperativeStickyAssignor — fed into PARTITION_ASSIGNMENT_STRATEGY_CONFIG. This lets you experiment with how partitions get distributed and whether rebalances are eager (revoke-all) or cooperative (incremental).

questions.question: With 3 consumers running and 6 partitions, you Ctrl-C one consumer. What do the survivors log?
alternatives: Nothing — the dead consumer's partitions go unprocessed
An error that the group is now under-provisioned
A REVOKED then ASSIGNED as they rebalance and pick up the orphaned partitions
They each spawn a thread to replace the dead consumer
questions.comment: Killing a consumer triggers a group rebalance. With the default eager RangeAssignor, survivors first get REVOKED (all partitions dropped) then ASSIGNED (new wider set), absorbing the dead consumer's partitions so processing continues — group state flips Rebalancing→Stable.

questions.question: Why does the producer call `producer.send(record).get()` instead of just `producer.send(record)`?
alternatives: `.get()` is required or the record is never actually sent
It blocks on the returned Future to get RecordMetadata (partition/offset) and send synchronously
It retries the send until it succeeds
It commits the producer's transaction
questions.comment: send() returns a Future<RecordMetadata>; calling `.get()` blocks until the broker acks and yields the actual partition and offset, which the demo prints. It also makes sends synchronous — simple and deterministic for the POC, though it sacrifices throughput versus async callbacks.

questions.question: What role does the kafka-ui service play, and how does it reach the broker?
alternatives: It is a second broker for replication, connecting on localhost:9092
A web UI on port 8080 that connects to the broker via the internal listener kafka:19092
A CLI tool that creates the topic on startup
The producer's REST gateway on port 9092
questions.comment: kafka-ui (kafbat/kafka-ui) exposes a dashboard on :8080 to watch topics, partitions, consumer groups and lag. It uses BOOTSTRAPSERVERS=kafka:19092 — the INTERNAL listener for container-to-container traffic — while host apps use the EXTERNAL localhost:9092.

questions.question: All consumers default to GROUP_ID=order-processors. If you instead gave each consumer a unique group id, what changes?
alternatives: Each group independently consumes every partition, so every consumer sees all 6 partitions and all messages
Only the first consumer receives messages, the rest are rejected
The topic must be recreated for new groups to attach
Ordering per customer is lost because keys are ignored
questions.comment: Partition splitting happens within a group. Separate group ids mean separate offset tracking, so each group gets a full copy of the stream — every consumer is assigned all 6 partitions. Sharing one group id is what makes them divide the partitions and scale out.
